notes: searches: ~11, clusters: 8 (early biography; Rome/Scala Sancta; Tetzel/indulgences; 95 theses posting+spread; Augsburg/Cajetan/Frederick; D'Aubigne quote cluster; Exsurge Domine/bull-burning; Reformation spread to countries). Common-knowledge fast-path applied to textbook Luther facts and to the four Bible-quotation-accuracy claims (043, 052, 128, 129 verified verbatim against KJV). Control sample of 3 needs_verification:false claims: GC-07-002, GC-07-040, GC-07-093 (each noted). Almost every attributed_quote traces to J.H. Merle D'Aubigne, History of the Reformation, White's own cited source per the 1911 Appendix refs 159-197; these are genuine Luther utterances as transmitted by the standard 19th-c. source, hence supported/medium unless the underlying episode is itself embellished (036/037) -> anachronistic. Tetzel polemical details (047, 050, 056) trace to D'Aubigne and are treated as anachronistic against modern scholarship.

GC-07-001.rationale: Theological claim: God accomplished a great work through Luther. Divine agency is not empirically checkable.

GC-07-002.rationale: [control sample: extractor flagged needs_verification=false] Interpretive/theological framing ('called to lead the church from the darkness of popery'). The embedded factual kernel - that Luther stood foremost among the leaders of the Protestant Reformation - is textbook common knowledge and true; the evaluative framing itself is unverifiable. Extractor's flag defensible.

GC-07-003.rationale: [common_knowledge] Luther was born into a family of humble peasant origin; his father Hans Luder rose from the peasantry into copper mining. Early years were modest.
GC-07-003.modern_consensus: Luther's paternal line was peasant stock; the family was of modest means in his childhood though the father later prospered.

GC-07-004.rationale: Hans Luther worked in copper mining/smelting and funded Martin's education. Confirmed by standard biography.
GC-07-004.modern_consensus: Hans Luther was a relatively successful miner and smelter who paid for his son's schooling.

sources.title: Martin Luther - Wikipedia
sources.note: Father a successful miner and smelter.

GC-07-005.rationale: [common_knowledge] Hans Luther intended his son for a legal career; Martin began law studies at Erfurt before entering the monastery.
GC-07-005.modern_consensus: Standard biography: father wanted Martin to become a lawyer.

GC-07-006.rationale: Theological claim about God's purpose for Luther. Not empirically checkable.

GC-07-007.rationale: Characterization of Hans Luther as strong-minded and resolute matches D'Aubigne and standard portraits; evaluative but consistent with the record.

GC-07-008.rationale: Hans Luther distrusted monasticism and was furious/displeased when Martin entered the Augustinian monastery in 1505 without consent.
GC-07-008.modern_consensus: Father was furious at what he saw as a waste of the education he had funded.

sources.title: Faces of the Reformation: Luther's Parents

GC-07-009.rationale: Reconciliation came roughly two years later, around Luther's ordination and first mass (1507), which his father attended; Hans still voiced reservations about the monastic choice.
GC-07-009.modern_consensus: Luther entered the monastery July 1505; reconciled with father by his first mass in 1507 (~2 years).

sources.title: Martin Luther's Life - Holy Trinity
sources.note: Father reconciled by 1507 first mass, ~2 years after 1505.

GC-07-010.rationale: Parents gave attention to religious upbringing; consistent with D'Aubigne and standard biography, though the household piety was strict.

GC-07-011.rationale: Luther recalled harsh corporal discipline at school (Mansfeld/Magdeburg); the 'harshness and even violence' matches his own recollections transmitted by D'Aubigne.

GC-07-012.rationale: As a schoolboy (Eisenach) Luther sang for bread door-to-door (a Kurrende chorister) and knew hunger; a well-attested biographical detail.

GC-07-013.rationale: Luther matriculated at the University of Erfurt in 1501; born Nov 1483 he was 17 turning 18 that year, so 'age eighteen' (from D'Aubigne) is off by at most a year - within period reckoning, not a substantive error.

sources.title: Martin Luther - Wikipedia
sources.note: In 1501, at age 17, entered University of Erfurt.

GC-07-014.rationale: By Luther's university years the father had prospered enough to support him; consistent with the record of Hans Luther's rising mining fortunes.

GC-07-015.rationale: Attributed maxim 'To pray well is the better half of study' traces to D'Aubigne (White's cited source); reflects a saying Luther is reported to have used at Erfurt.

sources.title: History of the Reformation (D'Aubigne) - CCEL
sources.note: White's cited source for Erfurt-period sayings.

GC-07-016.rationale: The tradition that Luther found a complete Latin Bible in the Erfurt library and was struck by it is standard biography; the stronger claim that he was 'ignorant even of its existence' is the traditional embellishment (he encountered scripture in liturgy), so accepted with medium confidence.
GC-07-016.modern_consensus: Luther discovered a complete Latin Bible in the Erfurt library; the 'never before seen / unaware of existence' framing is the traditional gloss.

sources.title: Luther finds the Latin Bible in the University Library at Erfurt - Library of Congress

GC-07-017.rationale: Exclamation 'O that God would give me such a book for myself!' traces to D'Aubigne, White's cited source (ref 160).

GC-07-018.rationale: Theological/supernatural claim: angels beside Luther and rays from God's throne. Not empirically checkable.

GC-07-019.rationale: Luther entered the Augustinian cloister (1505) out of spiritual anxiety over sin and salvation; GC omits the famous thunderstorm vow but the underlying religious motive is well-attested.
GC-07-019.modern_consensus: Luther entered monastery in 1505 after a thunderstorm vow, driven by fear for his soul/desire for salvation.

GC-07-020.rationale: As a novice/friar Luther performed menial duties and mendicant begging; consistent with Augustinian practice as transmitted by D'Aubigne.

GC-07-021.rationale: The chained convent Bible to which Luther repaired is a detail from D'Aubigne (White's cited source); consistent with monastic library practice.

GC-07-022.rationale: Luther's severe monastic asceticism - fasting, vigils, self-mortification seeking to subdue sin - is well-documented in his own later recollections.
GC-07-022.modern_consensus: Luther practiced extreme austerities as an Augustinian friar.

GC-07-023.rationale: Authentic Luther self-description ('I was indeed a pious monk...') as rendered by D'Aubigne (ref 161); reflects his repeated later statements about his monkish rigor.

GC-07-024.rationale: Luther's health suffered from his austerities, including fainting/weakness; consistent with biographical record.

GC-07-025.rationale: Johann von Staupitz, Luther's Augustinian superior, counseled him to turn from morbid self-examination toward Christ's mercy - a central, well-documented pastoral relationship.
GC-07-025.modern_consensus: Staupitz directed Luther away from anxious works-piety toward trust in Christ.

GC-07-026.rationale: Staupitz's counsel quote ('throw yourself into the Redeemer's arms...') traces to D'Aubigne (ref 162) and faithfully conveys Staupitz's known pastoral message.

GC-07-027.rationale: [common_knowledge] Luther was ordained priest (1507) and moved from the cloister to a professorship at the University of Wittenberg (1508, permanently 1511/12).

GC-07-028.rationale: At Wittenberg Luther lectured on the Psalms (1513-15), Romans (1515-16), Galatians and Hebrews, studying the biblical text closely; the 'original tongues' framing is broadly right though his Greek/Hebrew was still developing.
GC-07-028.modern_consensus: Luther lectured on Psalms, Romans, Galatians, Hebrews at Wittenberg.

GC-07-029.rationale: Staupitz pressed a reluctant Luther to preach and to take his doctorate; Luther's hesitation and sense of unworthiness are attested.

GC-07-030.rationale: Luther traveled on foot to Rome (1510-11) on Augustinian business, lodging in monasteries along the route.
GC-07-030.modern_consensus: Luther walked to Rome c.1510-1511, staying at monasteries.

sources.title: Martin Luther: His Trip to Rome (1510)

GC-07-031.rationale: Luther's shock at the wealth and luxury of Italian monasteries is recorded in his later recollections; transmitted here via D'Aubigne.

GC-07-032.rationale: Luther's greeting on first sighting Rome ('Salve, Sancta Roma!' / 'Holy Rome, I salute thee!') is a well-attested pilgrimage detail transmitted by D'Aubigne (ref 163).
GC-07-032.modern_consensus: Luther fell down and exclaimed 'Salve, Sancta Roma' on approaching Rome.

sources.title: Luther's Visit To Rome - Lineage Journey

GC-07-033.rationale: Luther's later reports of clerical irreverence and profanity during hurried masses in Rome are attested in his Table Talk and recollections.

GC-07-034.rationale: The 'If there is a hell, Rome is built over it' saying about Roman corruption traces to D'Aubigne (ref 164) and reflects Luther's later reported sentiments about Rome.

GC-07-035.rationale: An indulgence was attached to climbing the Scala Sancta ('Pilate's staircase') on one's knees; the staircase was traditionally said to have been brought from Jerusalem by St. Helena.
GC-07-035.modern_consensus: The Scala Sancta near the Lateran carried an indulgence for ascent on the knees; tradition attributes its transfer from Jerusalem to St. Helena c.326.

GC-07-036.rationale: The dramatic scene - a 'voice like thunder' proclaiming 'The just shall live by faith' (Rom 1:17) mid-ascent, sending Luther fleeing - is D'Aubigne's embellishment. Luther's own account is more modest ('who knows whether it is true?'), and his justification-by-faith breakthrough (the 'tower experience') is dated years later, c.1518-19, not to the staircase. GC follows the period-Protestant dramatization.
GC-07-036.modern_consensus: Historians treat the Scala Sancta thunderclap conversion as legendary; the Rom 1:17 breakthrough came later.
GC-07-036.period_historiography: D'Aubigne (History of the Reformation, White's ref 165) narrates the dramatic staircase episode with the thunder-voice.

sources.title: Scala Sancta - Wikipedia
sources.note: Luther's own recorded reaction was doubt ('who knows whether this is true'), not a thunderclap revelation.

sources.title: Martin Luther - Britannica
sources.note: Tower experience/justification breakthrough dated to Wittenberg years, not Rome.

GC-07-037.rationale: The retrospective claim that leaving Rome marked the inward beginning of Luther's break with the papacy dramatizes the staircase episode. Modern scholarship holds the Rome trip did not turn Luther against the papacy; his separation grew from the indulgence controversy (1517 on). Traces to D'Aubigne's narrative arc.
GC-07-037.modern_consensus: Luther's break with Rome originated in the 1517+ indulgence controversy, not disillusionment at Rome in 1510-11.
GC-07-037.period_historiography: D'Aubigne frames the Rome visit and staircase as the turning point of Luther's inward separation.

GC-07-038.rationale: [common_knowledge] After returning from Rome, Luther received the degree of Doctor of Theology (Divinity) at Wittenberg in 1512.
GC-07-038.modern_consensus: Luther made Doctor of Theology at Wittenberg, October 1512.

GC-07-039.rationale: Luther's doctoral oath bound him to teach the Scriptures faithfully; he later appealed to that oath as authorizing his stand on the Bible. The 'not the popes' gloss is D'Aubigne's framing but consistent with Luther's own retrospective use of the vow.

GC-07-040.rationale: [common_knowledge] [control sample: extractor flagged needs_verification=false] Although framed interpretively, the claim asserts a checkable historiographical proposition: the principle of scriptural authority (sola scriptura) was central to the Reformation and struck at papal supremacy. This is standard, uncontested Reformation historiography. Control note: the interpretive claim smuggles a supportable factual core.
GC-07-040.modern_consensus: Sola scriptura vs. papal/traditional authority is universally treated as the foundational principle of Luther's Reformation.

GC-07-041.rationale: Luther attacked scholastic philosophy/theology, notably in his 1517 Disputation Against Scholastic Theology, opposing Aristotle and the schoolmen.
GC-07-041.modern_consensus: Luther openly repudiated scholastic theology (Disputation Against Scholastic Theology, 1517).

GC-07-042.rationale: Interpretive/rhetorical claim (a metaphorical 'light' at Wittenberg extending to the ends of the earth and to the close of time). Not empirically checkable.

GC-07-043.rationale: [common_knowledge] Quotation-accuracy check only. 'I came not to send peace, but a sword' is Matthew 10:34 (KJV), quoted verbatim.

GC-07-044.rationale: Authentic Luther statement ('God does not guide me, He pushes me forward...') as transmitted by D'Aubigne (ref 167); a well-known Luther self-description.

GC-07-045.rationale: [common_knowledge] Indulgences were sold under papal authority (Leo X) ostensibly to fund the rebuilding of St. Peter's Basilica.
GC-07-045.modern_consensus: The 1515 indulgence (preached by Tetzel) was tied to St. Peter's construction, authorized by Leo X.

GC-07-046.rationale: [common_knowledge] The St. Peter's indulgence campaign directly provoked Luther's Ninety-Five Theses and the Reformation - a standard causal account.

GC-07-047.rationale: The claim that Tetzel had been convicted of grave offenses and escaped punishment is the Innsbruck adultery legend (a purported sentence of drowning/imprisonment for adultery, remitted by intercession). Even the 1911 Britannica hedges with 'is said to have been'; modern scholarship treats it as unverified Protestant polemic. Traces to D'Aubigne/Wylie.
GC-07-047.modern_consensus: The Innsbruck-adultery story is uncertain and likely polemical; scholars use qualified language.
GC-07-047.period_historiography: D'Aubigne and Wylie report Tetzel's prior conviction as fact; 1911 Britannica repeats it hedged.

sources.title: 1911 Encyclopaedia Britannica/Tetzel, Johann - Wikisource
sources.note: 'Is said to have been condemned' - qualified/uncertain.

sources.title: The Facts About Johann Tetzel (survey of scholarship)
sources.note: Position; catalogues the legendary status of the adultery story.

GC-07-048.rationale: Tetzel's indulgence preaching was extravagant and included exaggerated claims; the polemical framing ('glaring falsehoods, marvelous tales') is from D'Aubigne but the substance of hyperbolic preaching is attested.

sources.title: Myths & Facts Re: Tetzel & Indulgences
sources.note: Position; even critics of the legends concede extravagant preaching.

GC-07-049.rationale: The ceremonial announcement preceding Tetzel's entry into a town ('The grace of God and of the holy father is at your gates') traces to D'Aubigne (ref 170) and matches the pageantry of the indulgence campaign.

GC-07-050.rationale: The claim that Tetzel's certificates forgave future sins and that 'not even repentance is necessary' is polemical exaggeration. Catholic indulgence doctrine required contrition/confession; forgiveness of future sins was never Church teaching. GC follows D'Aubigne's polemical rendering (ref 171).
GC-07-050.modern_consensus: Indulgences remitted temporal penalty for already-confessed sin, not future sin, and did not dispense with repentance; the contrary claim is polemical.
GC-07-050.period_historiography: D'Aubigne attributes the 'future sins / no repentance needed' claims to Tetzel.

sources.title: Questions & Answers Concerning Indulgences - Famous Trials
sources.note: Indulgences presuppose contrition/confession; do not license future sin.

sources.title: Myths & Facts Re: Tetzel & Indulgences
sources.note: Position; catalogues the 'future sins' claim as a polemical distortion.

GC-07-051.rationale: The 'coin in the coffer / soul springs from purgatory' teaching cannot be traced verbatim to Tetzel's extant sermons (the jingle predates him, Univ. of Paris complaint 1482), but Martin Brecht and others hold he taught this in substance - that an indulgence for the dead was infallibly applied on payment. GC's paraphrase captures the substance he preached.
GC-07-051.modern_consensus: The exact jingle is not provably Tetzel's, but he taught the underlying idea of immediate release of a soul from purgatory upon payment.

sources.title: Martin Brecht: Tetzel and the coffer saying (scholarship survey)
sources.note: Position; cites Brecht that Tetzel taught the substance though the exact jingle is not his.

sources.title: Questions & Answers Concerning Indulgences - Famous Trials

GC-07-052.rationale: [common_knowledge] Quotation-accuracy check only. 'Thy money perish with thee, because thou hast thought that the gift of God may be purchased with money' is Acts 8:20 (KJV), quoted verbatim; the Simon Magus context is correct.

GC-07-053.rationale: The indulgence campaign was lucrative and widely purchased; 'eager thousands' and revenue flowing in is consistent with the documented success of the sales.

GC-07-054.rationale: Some learned Catholics privately doubted indulgence claims, yet no prelate openly opposed the traffic before Luther; broadly consistent with the historical situation.

GC-07-055.rationale: Parishioners at Wittenberg who had bought Tetzel's letters came to Luther expecting absolution; his refusal and insistence on true repentance is a documented spark of the controversy.
GC-07-055.modern_consensus: Luther's pastoral encounter with indulgence-holders in his parish helped prompt the Ninety-Five Theses.

GC-07-056.rationale: The vivid detail of Tetzel enraged, lighting fires in public squares, and quoting 'an order from the pope to burn all heretics' traces to D'Aubigne (ref 175). Tetzel did respond angrily and issued counter-theses (later burned by students), but the specific 'burn heretics' quote and public bonfires are period-polemical dramatization, not independently documented.
GC-07-056.period_historiography: D'Aubigne narrates Tetzel's rage and the 'burn heretics' threat.

sources.title: 1911 Encyclopaedia Britannica/Tetzel, Johann - Wikisource
sources.note: Documents Tetzel's counter-attack on Luther without the bonfire/burn-heretics theatrics.

GC-07-057.rationale: Luther preached from his own experience of failed works-righteousness and peace found in Christ; consistent with his documented preaching and self-testimony.

GC-07-058.rationale: The Castle Church of Wittenberg (All Saints') held Frederick the Wise's large relic collection, displayed on holy days with attached indulgences/remission for viewing.
GC-07-058.modern_consensus: Frederick's relic collection at the Castle Church carried indulgences shown on All Saints' Day.

GC-07-059.rationale: Luther presented the Ninety-Five Theses against indulgences on the eve/day of All Saints (31 Oct 1517), traditionally by posting on the Castle Church door and offering academic disputation. His letter to Archbishop Albert that day is certain; the physical door-posting is the traditional account questioned by some scholars (Iserloh) but still mainstream. Extractor correctly notes the year is supplied as context.
GC-07-059.modern_consensus: 31 Oct 1517 is the accepted date; the door-posting is traditional and debated (Iserloh thesis) but broadly accepted; the mailing to Albert is documented.

sources.title: 1517 Luther Posts the 95 Theses - Christian History Institute

GC-07-060.rationale: The Theses argued that the pope could only declare/remit penalties he himself or canon law imposed and had no power over divine guilt or purgatorial penalty (theses 5-6, 20-21); GC's summary is broadly accurate though compressed.
GC-07-060.modern_consensus: The Theses limited papal power over the remission of penalty and denied papal power over guilt.

GC-07-061.rationale: Aided by printing, the Theses spread across Germany within about two weeks and across Europe within roughly a month/two months; GC's 'few days'/'few weeks' is a slight compression but substantively correct.
GC-07-061.modern_consensus: Copies spread through Germany in ~2 weeks and through Europe within a month or two.

sources.title: The Reformation in Germany: Luther and the Printing Press

GC-07-062.rationale: Luther's response to charges of pride ('a man rarely puts forth any new idea without... being accused of exciting quarrels... Christ and all the martyrs') traces to D'Aubigne (White's source cluster, ref 176 region) and reflects Luther's documented rhetoric.

GC-07-063.rationale: 'If the work be of God, who shall stop it?...Thy will, O holy Father' traces to D'Aubigne (ref 176); an authentic expression of Luther's providentialism echoing his letters.

GC-07-064.rationale: Interpretive-causal claim that many church/state figures recognized the theses' truth but resisted because reform threatened revenue/authority; a defensible characterization of the reaction, consistent with the financial stakes of the indulgence trade.

GC-07-065.rationale: Luther's retrospective ('Who was I to oppose the majesty of the pope... what my heart suffered during these first two years... despair') echoes his 1545 autobiographical preface; transmitted here via D'Aubigne (ref 177).

GC-07-066.rationale: Luther's counsel that Scripture is grasped through prayer, not intellect alone, traces to D'Aubigne (ref 178) and matches his known theology of Scripture (oratio, meditatio, tentatio).

GC-07-067.rationale: The cry of Roman zealots calling Luther a heretic deserving the scaffold traces to D'Aubigne (ref 179); representative of the hostile reaction, though a composite attributed exclamation rather than a single documented speaker.

GC-07-068.rationale: Theological/supernatural claim: angels sent to protect Luther. Not empirically checkable.

GC-07-069.rationale: Early adherents of the Reformation did suffer persecution and martyrdom (e.g., the first Lutheran martyrs at Brussels, 1523); the statement is generally true though placed early in the narrative.

GC-07-070.rationale: Luther's sermons and writings drew wide attention across Germany and awakened many; well-documented by the rapid growth of his following.

GC-07-071.rationale: Luther was cited to appear at Rome in 1518 on a charge of heresy; the citation was subsequently commuted to a hearing in Germany.
GC-07-071.modern_consensus: Luther received a summons to Rome (1518), later changed to an examination before Cajetan at Augsburg.

sources.title: Luther and Cajetan (Schaff, History of the Christian Church vol. VII)

GC-07-072.rationale: Luther's protectors (notably Frederick the Wise and the university) secured that his examination be held in Germany rather than Rome.

GC-07-073.rationale: The pope appointed Cardinal Cajetan as legate to hear the case; the papal instructions/brief treated Luther as already a notorious suspect of heresy.
GC-07-073.modern_consensus: Cajetan's instructions presumed Luther's guilt and required recantation.

GC-07-074.rationale: The severe language of the papal briefs to Cajetan (prosecute/constrain without delay; proscribe, banish, curse, excommunicate his adherents) is quoted here via D'Aubigne (ref 180) and reflects the harsh papal instructions of Aug 1518.

GC-07-075.rationale: The papal instructions directed excommunication of any who failed to seize Luther and his adherents, sparing the emperor; consistent with the sweeping terms of the 1518 briefs.

GC-07-076.rationale: Interpretive/polemical evaluation ('the true spirit of popery... not a trace of Christian principle'). A value judgment, not an empirically checkable proposition.

GC-07-077.rationale: The factual core - that the papal instructions pronounced Luther a heretic before any hearing, with no chance to defend himself - is consistent with Cajetan's brief presuming guilt. The rhetorical amplification is interpretive.

GC-07-078.rationale: Philip Melanchthon arrived at Wittenberg in 1518 (as professor of Greek) and quickly won admiration; he became Luther's close collaborator.
GC-07-078.modern_consensus: Melanchthon came to Wittenberg in 1518 and became Luther's leading ally.

GC-07-079.rationale: Melanchthon became Luther's most trusted colleague, his systematic caution complementing Luther's boldness - a standard characterization of the Wittenberg partnership.

GC-07-080.rationale: Augsburg was the appointed place of Luther's examination before Cajetan (Oct 1518); Luther traveled there (partly on foot) amid fears for his safety.
GC-07-080.modern_consensus: Luther went to Augsburg to meet Cajetan in October 1518.

GC-07-081.rationale: Luther's 'I am like Jeremiah, a man of strife...let them take my body...' traces to D'Aubigne (ref 181) and reflects his documented resolve on the eve of Augsburg.

GC-07-082.rationale: Luther initially lacked a safe-conduct at Augsburg; his friends urged him not to appear without one, and an imperial safe-conduct was obtained.
GC-07-082.modern_consensus: Luther waited for a safe-conduct before meeting Cajetan.

sources.title: Martin Luther at Augsburg in 1518 - Steadfast Lutherans

GC-07-083.rationale: Cajetan's aim was to force retraction or, failing that, to have Luther sent to Rome; the parallel to the fate of Huss and Jerome (burned at Constance) is the natural fear given Luther's own comparisons.

GC-07-084.rationale: Luther received the safe-conduct (11 Oct 1518) before appearing before Cajetan the next day.
GC-07-084.modern_consensus: Luther got the letter of safe-conduct Oct 11, appeared before Cajetan Oct 12.

GC-07-085.rationale: Cajetan received Luther with professed fatherly friendliness but demanded unconditional submission and recantation without debate.
GC-07-085.modern_consensus: Cajetan demanded retraction and submission to the pope, refusing disputation.

GC-07-086.rationale: Luther expressed willingness to be corrected, offered to submit to universities, and protested being required to recant without being shown his error - consistent with the Augsburg record.

GC-07-087.rationale: Cajetan repeatedly demanded 'Retract!' (revoca) without engaging Luther's arguments; the single-word demand is well-attested (Luther: 'I would be most welcome if I but spoke revoco').

GC-07-088.rationale: Luther obtained permission to submit a written response after the oral conference proved fruitless; consistent with the sequence of the Augsburg interviews.

GC-07-089.rationale: Luther's reasoning about the 'double gain' of a written answer and the 'arrogant and babbling despot' traces to D'Aubigne (ref 182); reflects his correspondence during Augsburg.

GC-07-090.rationale: Luther presented a scripture-based written statement; Cajetan dismissed it and refused genuine engagement, matching the record of the third interview.

GC-07-091.rationale: Cajetan's threat to send Luther to Rome and to excommunicate him and his partisans, ending 'Retract, or return no more' (revoca or come back no more), traces to D'Aubigne (ref 183) and matches the breakdown of the interviews.

GC-07-092.rationale: Luther withdrew with his companions, signaling that no recantation would follow; consistent with the end of the Augsburg conference.

GC-07-093.rationale: [control sample: extractor flagged needs_verification=false] Partisan character-portrait of the two parties at Augsburg (humble Luther vs. haughty legate). The evaluative contrast is interpretive/unverifiable. Control note: the embedded factual kernel - that Cajetan offered no scriptural refutation and simply demanded retraction - is supported by the Augsburg record (see GC-07-085/087). Extractor's flag defensible.

GC-07-094.rationale: Luther left Augsburg secretly before dawn (20 Oct 1518) on horseback with a guide, escaping through a small gate in the city wall.
GC-07-094.modern_consensus: Luther fled Augsburg by night through a gate/opening in the wall on horseback.

sources.title: Martin Luther at Augsburg in 1518 - Steadfast Lutherans

GC-07-095.rationale: Luther's flight put him beyond reach before Cajetan acted; consistent with the accounts of his sudden nighttime departure.

GC-07-096.rationale: After Augsburg, Cajetan wrote to Frederick the Wise denouncing Luther and demanding that Frederick send him to Rome or banish him.
GC-07-096.modern_consensus: Cajetan pressured Frederick to arrest/send Luther to Rome or expel him.

sources.title: Martin Luther at Augsburg in 1518 - Steadfast Lutherans

GC-07-097.rationale: Luther repeatedly offered to recant if shown his errors from Scripture; a consistent feature of his defense at and after Augsburg.

GC-07-098.rationale: Frederick's reply to Cajetan (18 Dec 1518) declined to act because Luther had not been convicted of error, noting that his learned men did not judge Luther's doctrine heretical; the quote (via D'Aubigne ref 184) matches Frederick's documented letter.
GC-07-098.modern_consensus: Frederick wrote Cajetan he would send Luther to Rome only after conviction of heresy.

sources.title: Martin Luther at Augsburg in 1518 - Steadfast Lutherans
sources.note: Frederick balked; would send Luther only after conviction.

GC-07-099.rationale: Frederick the Wise refused both to send Luther to Rome and to expel him from Saxony - a decisive act of protection.
GC-07-099.modern_consensus: Frederick protected Luther, refusing to hand him over or banish him.

sources.title: Martin Luther at Augsburg in 1518 - Steadfast Lutherans

GC-07-100.rationale: Frederick's cautious sympathy for reform is well-attested, but his interior motive ('secretly rejoiced', perceiving a general moral breakdown) is an inference about his private mind; the outward protection is documented, the inner state is only weakly checkable.

sources.title: Martin Luther - Wikipedia
sources.note: Frederick's protective sympathy attested; inner motive interpretive.

GC-07-101.rationale: A marked decline in relic-pilgrimage at the Castle Church within about a year of the Theses is reported by D'Aubigne (ref 185 vicinity) and is consistent with the swift erosion of indulgence/relic devotion in Wittenberg.

GC-07-102.rationale: Luther's fame drew a surge of students to Wittenberg from across Germany and beyond; university enrollment rose sharply in these years.
GC-07-102.modern_consensus: Wittenberg's enrollment grew markedly as students flocked to Luther and Melanchthon.

GC-07-103.rationale: The description of students raising their hands and praising God at the sight of Wittenberg traces to D'Aubigne (ref 185); a rhetorical report of the university's magnetism.

GC-07-104.rationale: Luther's line that reading the papal decretals left him unsure 'whether the pope is antichrist himself, or his apostle' traces to D'Aubigne (ref 186) and matches his documented radicalization c.1519-20.

GC-07-105.rationale: At this stage (c.1518-19) Luther still saw himself as a loyal Catholic seeking internal reform, not yet intending schism; a standard reading of his early development.
GC-07-105.modern_consensus: Luther initially sought reform from within and did not plan to leave the Church.

GC-07-106.rationale: By 1519-1520 Luther's writings circulated to Switzerland, the Low Countries, France, Italy, England and Spain via the printing trade. The rhetorical 'received as the word of life' in England overstates the mixed/hostile official reception (Henry VIII wrote against Luther in 1521), but the geographic spread is documented.
GC-07-106.modern_consensus: Luther's works reached France, England, Italy, the Netherlands, Switzerland and Spain by 1519-1520, though reception varied and was often suppressed.

sources.title: The rise of Protestantism in France (1520-1562) - Musee protestant

GC-07-107.rationale: Extremist opponents did declare that killing Luther would be no sin; such incitements are reported by D'Aubigne (ref vicinity) and reflect the venom of the polemic against him.

GC-07-108.rationale: The anecdote of a stranger with a concealed pistol fleeing at Luther's 'I am in God's hands' reply (ref 187) comes from D'Aubigne and cannot be independently corroborated; recorded as a period source-anecdote, not independently established.

GC-07-109.rationale: The quoted phrase describing Luther's doctrines heard 'in cottages and convents... in the palaces of kings' traces to D'Aubigne (ref vicinity); a rhetorical but broadly accurate report of the reach of his teaching.

GC-07-110.rationale: Around the 1519 Leipzig Disputation and after, Luther read Hus and found in him the doctrine of justification by faith he was defending; well-documented.
GC-07-110.modern_consensus: Luther read Hus c.1519-1520 and recognized his own convergence with Hus.

sources.title: The Long Legacy of a Roasted Goose - Christian History Institute

GC-07-111.rationale: Genuine and famous Luther statement. Writing to Spalatin in February 1520 Luther declared 'sumus omnes Hussitae ignorantes' - 'We are all Hussites without knowing it,' adding that Paul and Augustine were in effect Hussites. GC's rendering (via D'Aubigne ref 188) is accurate.
GC-07-111.modern_consensus: Luther wrote 'We are all Hussites without knowing it' to Spalatin, Feb 1520.

sources.title: The Long Legacy of a Roasted Goose - Christian History Institute
sources.note: 'sumus omnes Hussitae ignorantes', letter to Spalatin Feb 1520.

GC-07-112.rationale: Luther wrote 'To the Christian Nobility of the German Nation' (Aug 1520), an appeal to the emperor and German nobility for reform of Christendom.

sources.title: An Open Letter to the Christian Nobility - Project Wittenberg

GC-07-113.rationale: The passage contrasting the pope's magnificence with 'the poor Jesus' and 'My kingdom is not of this world' reflects Luther's argument in the Address to the German Nobility; rendered here via D'Aubigne (ref 189).

sources.title: An Open Letter to the Christian Nobility - Project Wittenberg

GC-07-114.rationale: The 'great gates of hell' warning about universities that neglect Scripture is a well-known passage from Luther's Address to the Christian Nobility (1520); rendered via D'Aubigne (ref 190).

sources.title: An Open Letter to the Christian Nobility - Project Wittenberg

GC-07-115.rationale: The Address to the Christian Nobility circulated rapidly (its first printing of ~4,000 copies sold out within days) and had a powerful national impact.
GC-07-115.modern_consensus: The 1520 Address was a runaway bestseller that stirred German opinion.

GC-07-116.rationale: The bull Exsurge Domine (15 June 1520) condemned Luther's teachings and gave him sixty days after its publication in Saxony to recant on pain of excommunication.

GC-07-117.rationale: Medieval papal excommunication (and interdict) could indeed cow monarchs and isolate the condemned; the general historical characterization is sound, if rhetorically heightened.

GC-07-118.rationale: Luther's calm defiance ('not a leaf falls without the will of our Father... light thing to die for the Word') traces to D'Aubigne (ref 191) and matches his documented resolve facing the bull.

GC-07-119.rationale: Luther's declaration that the bull was impious and false and that 'the pope is antichrist, and his throne that of Satan' traces to D'Aubigne (ref 192) and matches his writings against the bull (e.g., Against the Execrable Bull of Antichrist).

GC-07-120.rationale: Many sympathizers were intimidated by the papal decree and reluctant to risk their lives; a fair characterization of the mixed public response to Exsurge Domine.

GC-07-121.rationale: On 10 December 1520 at the Elster Gate in Wittenberg Luther publicly burned Exsurge Domine together with volumes of canon law and decretals, before students and townsfolk.
GC-07-121.modern_consensus: Luther burned the bull and canon law at Wittenberg, 10 Dec 1520.

GC-07-122.rationale: Luther's explanation for burning the books ('a serious struggle has just begun... hitherto I have been only playing with the pope') traces to D'Aubigne (ref 193) and reflects his stance after the burning.

GC-07-123.rationale: Luther's 'Moses... Elijah... Isaiah... Ezekiel... alone' self-comparison, asserting the word of God was with him, traces to D'Aubigne (ref 194) and reflects his documented rhetoric of standing alone with Scripture.

GC-07-124.rationale: Luther's account of the inner struggle to stand alone against the pope and to call him antichrist, until Scripture fortified him, traces to D'Aubigne (ref 195) and matches his own retrospective testimony.

GC-07-125.rationale: The bull Decet Romanum Pontificem (3 Jan 1521) formally excommunicated Luther, confirming his separation from the Roman Church and condemning his adherents.
GC-07-125.modern_consensus: Decet Romanum Pontificem, Jan 1521, excommunicated Luther.

sources.title: Exsurge Domine - Wikipedia
sources.note: Formal excommunication followed via Decet Romanum Pontificem, 3 Jan 1521.

GC-07-126.rationale: Theological generalization about those God employs. Not empirically checkable.

GC-07-127.rationale: Theological claim about the cosmic conflict between Christ and Satan intensifying to the end of history. Not empirically checkable.

GC-07-128.rationale: [common_knowledge] Quotation-accuracy check only. The passage is John 15:19-20 (KJV), quoted verbatim ('If ye were of the world... The servant is not greater than his lord... If they have persecuted me, they will also persecute you').

GC-07-129.rationale: [common_knowledge] Quotation-accuracy check only. 'Woe unto you, when all men shall speak well of you! for so did their fathers to the false prophets' is Luke 6:26 (KJV), quoted verbatim.

GC-07-130.rationale: Interpretive/theological claim about the perennial enmity of 'the spirit of the world' toward the pure word of God. Not empirically checkable.
